question.text: During a New Fund Offer (NFO) of a mutual fund scheme, units are typically allotted to investors at:

answer.text: Face value, typically Rs. 10 per unit
NAV of an existing similar scheme of the same AMC
A premium determined by the demand during the NFO period
A price decided by SEBI based on the scheme's investment objective

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** The NFO price is usually the face value, typically Rs. 10 per unit. The NFO (except ELSS) will be open for a period of 15 days. Units are allotted within 5 business days of the NFO closing date. Subsequent ongoing purchases in open-ended schemes are at NAV-linked prices.
**The Trap:** NAV of existing schemes is irrelevant for a new scheme's NFO pricing. There is no premium-based pricing like IPOs. SEBI does not determine the price — the face value of Rs. 10 is standard across the industry.
**Cross-Exam Context:** MF topics overlap with Series V-A, V-B. NFO process and pricing are basic concepts tested in all MF certification exams.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Many investors think buying at Rs. 10 NFO price is "cheaper" than buying an existing fund at Rs. 50 NAV. Educate them that NAV is just a per-unit measure — what matters is the fund's future performance, not its starting price.
